items.q: Which component handles visual and spatial information?
items.why: Students mix up the slave systems. The phonological loop is for auditory/verbal information; the sketchpad is for visual/spatial. The central executive coordinates but doesn't store.

items.q: Asch's line study primarily demonstrates which type of conformity?
items.correct: Normative social influence (compliance to fit in).
items.misconception: Informational social influence (believing others know better).
items.why: Students assume participants genuinely believed the wrong line was correct. Asch's debriefs showed they knew it was wrong but conformed publicly to avoid rejection - this is normative, not informational.

items.q: In Pavlov's experiment, what is the conditioned response?
items.correct: Salivation to the bell alone, after pairing with food.
items.misconception: Salivation to the food (which is automatic).
items.random_wrong: The dog ringing the bell itself.
items.why: Students confuse the unconditioned response (automatic salivation to food) with the conditioned response (learned salivation to bell). The conditioned response is the LEARNED one.

items.q: Asch's conformity rate to the wrong answer was around:
items.why: It's 33% trials. “10%” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Berry's strategy combining home and host cultures is:
items.why: Correct answer: Integration. “Assimilation” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Year of Asch's classic conformity study:
items.why: The real figure is 1951. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
